quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Distillation"?

options.text: ΔTf = iKfm; solute particles lower the freezing point by disrupting crystal formation
Separation of a liquid mixture by selective evaporation and condensation; components separate based on boiling point differences
Electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy, from lowest to highest
The attraction between an ion and a polar molecule; the dominant force when ionic compounds dissolve in water

quiz_questions.explanation: "Distillation" is defined as: Separation of a liquid mixture by selective evaporation and condensation; components separate based on boiling point differences. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Chromatography"?

options.text: Acid: electron pair acceptor; base: electron pair donor; the broadest definition, encompassing reactions without proton transfer
An acid that can donate more than one proton: diprotic (H₂SO₄, H₂CO₃) or triprotic (H₃PO₄)
Separation based on differential partitioning between a stationary phase and a mobile phase; types include paper, thin-layer (TLC), gas (GC), and HPLC
A balanced equation that includes the enthalpy change (ΔH); coefficients represent moles, and the physical state of each species must be specified

quiz_questions.explanation: "Chromatography" is defined as: Separation based on differential partitioning between a stationary phase and a mobile phase; types include paper, thin-layer (TLC), gas (GC), and HPLC. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Filtration"?

options.text: A model stating reactions occur when molecules collide with sufficient energy (≥ Ea) and proper orientation
Equilibrium involving species in more than one phase; pure solids and liquids have activity = 1 and are excluded from K
Separation of an insoluble solid from a liquid using a filter; gravity filtration or vacuum filtration for faster processing
ΔH for any reaction equals the sum of ΔH values for the steps into which the reaction can be divided, regardless of path

quiz_questions.explanation: "Filtration" is defined as: Separation of an insoluble solid from a liquid using a filter; gravity filtration or vacuum filtration for faster processing. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Fractional distillation"?

options.text: Calculated the same way as K but with current concentrations; Q  K: reverse favored; Q = K: at equilibrium
Distillation using a fractionating column to achieve better separation of liquids with similar boiling points through repeated vaporization-condensation cycles
The rate at a specific moment, determined by the slope of the tangent to the concentration-vs.-time curve
The state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal; concentrations are constant but molecular-level reactions continue

quiz_questions.explanation: "Fractional distillation" is defined as: Distillation using a fractionating column to achieve better separation of liquids with similar boiling points through repeated vaporization-condensation cycles. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Centrifugation"?

options.text: Separation based on density differences using rapid spinning; denser particles settle to the bottom (pellet), lighter components remain in solution (supernatant)
-log[H⁺]; a logarithmic scale where each unit represents a 10-fold change in [H⁺]; pH 3 is 1000× more acidic than pH 6
The smallest repeating unit of a crystal lattice; common types include simple cubic, body-centered cubic (BCC), and face-centered cubic (FCC)
A substance that increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower Ea; regenerated and not consumed

quiz_questions.explanation: "Centrifugation" is defined as: Separation based on density differences using rapid spinning; denser particles settle to the bottom (pellet), lighter components remain in solution (supernatant). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Separation of a liquid mixture by selective evaporation and condensation; components separate based on boiling point differences"?

options.text: E°cell
Distillation
Calorimetry
Ka (acid dissociation constant)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Distillation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Separation based on differential partitioning between a stationary phase and a mobile phase; types include paper, thin-layer (TLC), gas (GC), and HPLC"?

options.text: Chromatography
Stoichiometric coefficient
Isotope
Half-reaction method

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Chromatography".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Separation of an insoluble solid from a liquid using a filter; gravity filtration or vacuum filtration for faster processing"?

options.text: Catalyst
Isotope
pKa
Filtration

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Filtration".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Distillation using a fractionating column to achieve better separation of liquids with similar boiling points through repeated vaporization-condensation cycles"?

options.text: Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution
Fractional distillation
Selective precipitation
Crystalline solid

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Fractional distillation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Separation based on density differences using rapid spinning; denser particles settle to the bottom (pellet), lighter components remain in solution (supernatant)"?

options.text: Colligative properties
Intermediate
Centrifugation
Gibbs free energy (ΔG)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Centrifugation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Separation Techniques is accurate?

options.text: Separation Techniques exploit differences in physical properties to isolate components of mixtures.
Strong acids (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃) and strong bases (NaOH, KOH) dissociate completely; weak acids/bases partially dissociate with Ka or Kb values.

This delocalized electron model explains metallic properties: electrical conductivity (electrons flow freely), thermal conductivity (electrons transfer kinetic energy), malleability and ductility (layers of cations slide without breaking bonds),...
Each elementary step has a molecularity (unimolecular, bimolecular, rarely termolecular) that directly determines its rate law.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Separation Techniques as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Separation Techniques?

options.text: Molecular polarity depends on both bond polarity and geometry.
Distillation separates liquids by boiling point differences, chromatography separates by differential affinity for stationary and mobile phases, and filtration separates solids from liquids.
Orbitals are filled in order of increasing energy: 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, 4p...
Zero-order: rate is independent of concentration; first-order: rate doubles when concentration doubles; second-order: rate quadruples when concentration doubles.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Separation Techniques as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Separation Techniques?

options.text: The 5% approximation simplifies calculations when [HA]₀/Ka > 400.
These techniques are fundamental to chemical analysis, purification, and quality control.
pH and pOH provide logarithmic scales for expressing H⁺ and OH⁻ concentrations. pH = -log[H⁺], pOH = -log[OH⁻], and pH + pOH = 14.00 at 25°C.
Stoichiometric calculations convert between grams, moles, and particles using molar mass and mole ratios from balanced equations.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Separation Techniques as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Separation Techniques?

options.text: The choice of method depends on the properties of the mixture components and the purity required.
This allows calculation of ΔH for reactions that are difficult to measure directly, by combining known reactions.
Activation energy (Ea) is the energy barrier that must be overcome for a reaction to proceed.
Intermolecular Forces (IMFs) are attractions between molecules that determine physical properties like boiling point, viscosity, and surface tension.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Separation Techniques as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Separation Techniques?

options.text: This delocalized electron model explains metallic properties: electrical conductivity (electrons flow freely), thermal conductivity (electrons transfer kinetic energy), malleability and ductility (layers of cations slide without breaking bonds),...
Separation Techniques exploit differences in physical properties to isolate components of mixtures.
The common-ion effect reduces solubility when a solution already contains one of the ions.
Electrochemistry and Redox connects chemical reactions to electrical energy.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Separation Techniques as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Separation Techniques?

options.text: Blood buffering: CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ HCO₃⁻ + H⁺ with specific K values maintaining blood pH in the narrow range of 7.35-7.45
Dissolution of ammonium nitrate: endothermic (ΔH > 0) but spontaneous because the large increase in entropy (ΔS >> 0) from ions dispersing in solution makes ΔG < 0
Diamond vs. graphite: same element (carbon) with different covalent bonding networks—diamond (4 bonds, 3D tetrahedral) is the hardest material, graphite (3 bonds, layered sheets) is a lubricant
Petroleum refining using fractional distillation to separate crude oil into fractions: gases, gasoline, kerosene, diesel, lubricating oils, and asphalt

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Separation Techniques.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Separation Techniques?

options.text: Water purification combining filtration (removing particles), activated carbon adsorption (removing organic compounds), and distillation or reverse osmosis (removing dissolved salts)

Tempered safety glass as an amorphous solid that, when broken, shatters into small granular pieces rather than sharp shards due to balanced internal stresses from thermal treatment (distinct from laminated glass, which holds together via a...
Industrial removal of products: in esterification (alcohol + acid ⇌ ester + water), removing water with molecular sieves shifts equilibrium toward ester, increasing yield
Electroplating using electrolysis: depositing a thin layer of metal (chrome, gold, nickel) onto objects for corrosion protection or aesthetics

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Separation Techniques.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Separation Techniques applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Greenhouse gas behavior: CO₂ (linear, no permanent dipole) still absorbs IR radiation through asymmetric stretching, while symmetric stretching is IR-inactive
Hard water treatment: Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions forming insoluble CaCO₃ scale in pipes and water heaters when Qsp exceeds Ksp upon heating
Diamond-to-graphite conversion: thermodynamically spontaneous (ΔG < 0) but kinetically extremely slow—diamonds are 'metastable' and effectively permanent
Forensic analysis using gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) to identify and quantify drugs, poisons, and accelerants in criminal investigations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Separation Techniques.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Separation Techniques?

options.text: Petroleum refining using fractional distillation to separate crude oil into fractions: gases, gasoline, kerosene, diesel, lubricating oils, and asphalt
Ammonia synthesis economics: K for N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ is large at low T (favors products) but reaction is too slow; industrial compromise at ~450°C, 200 atm gives usable rate and yield
Blood buffering: CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ HCO₃⁻ + H⁺ with specific K values maintaining blood pH in the narrow range of 7.35-7.45
Ammonia's pyramidal geometry (sp³, one lone pair) making it a strong Lewis base and critical industrial chemical in fertilizer production (Haber process)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Separation Techniques.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Separation Techniques in practice?

options.text: Radiocarbon dating using the ¹⁴C isotope to determine the age of organic materials up to ~50,000 years old
Hard water treatment: Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions forming insoluble CaCO₃ scale in pipes and water heaters when Qsp exceeds Ksp upon heating
Biological research: phosphate buffer (H₂PO₄⁻/HPO₄²⁻, pKa = 7.20) commonly used in cell culture media to maintain physiological pH
Water purification combining filtration (removing particles), activated carbon adsorption (removing organic compounds), and distillation or reverse osmosis (removing dissolved salts)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Separation Techniques.
